narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid family background in the high income bracket, you've put significant effort into your law school journey, including scoring a strong 38 on your LSAT and opting for part-time enrollment, which is less common among the vast majority of full-time students in our dataset. However, the admissions model, trained on thousands of past students' paths to the bar exam, has predicted a 41.1% probability that you would fail the bar on your first try—a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger projected odds of passing. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the typical student's around the middle of the pack, signaling to the model a potential dip in later academic momentum. This outweighed your impressive LSAT score, which was above the average of most applicants and strongly pulled the prediction in the favorable direction toward passing. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed somewhat toward the failure side, being well below the dataset norm and highlighting early challenges in your undergraduate performance relative to peers. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, while under the average 3.21, still nudged things positively, as did your high family income and part-time status, both of which the model views as supportive factors compared to typical students. Overall, your profile stands out for strengths like the LSAT and background but is held back by those lower decile rankings when stacked against students who generally climb higher in school standings. To move forward, I recommend focusing on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits or additional coursework, perhaps retaking relevant exams to demonstrate upward trends, and reapplying once you've strengthened those areas—many students in similar spots have turned things around with persistence. You're clearly capable, given your LSAT edge, so don't lose heart; this is just one model's snapshot of your potential bar exam path. With some strategic improvements, your next application could tell a different story. Keep pushing forward—we're rooting for you.
